cue: How do you describe someone with their head in the clouds?

es.literal: to have birds in the head
es.meaning: To be full of unrealistic notions; to be flighty.
es.example: Tiene pájaros en la cabeza: quiere renunciar y vivir en la playa.
es.exampleTranslation: She's got her head in the clouds — she wants to quit and live at the beach.

it.literal: to have crickets in the head
it.meaning: To have strange or unrealistic ideas; to be restless.
it.example: Ha grilli per la testa: vuole mollare tutto e partire.
it.exampleTranslation: He's full of wild ideas — he wants to drop everything and leave.

bridge.note: Both fill the head with small restless creatures, and the choice of creature is the whole difference. Spanish releases BIRDS — they fly off, romantic, headed somewhere. Italian releases CRICKETS — they jump and chirp and go nowhere, which is a sharper verdict: not dreaming, just noisy. Cross-reference 0001, where Spanish puts the whole person in the clouds instead.

interference.es_to_it: 'Avere uccelli per la testa' is not Italian — the head hosts grilli, and note the preposition: PER la testa, not nella.
interference.it_to_es: 'Tener grillos en la cabeza' is not the Spanish idiom; Spanish keeps birds — and uses EN, not por.

review.notes: Discovered via an Instagram card set Joe shared 2026-07-20 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). ES counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Bird-vs-cricket is a verdict difference, not just imagery.
